title: Siting and setting up a pig arc
subtitle: Choosing the right arc, positioning it for drainage and shade, and bedding it for a first batch of weaners.
excerpt: A pig arc is the standard outdoor housing for a pair of weaners or a single sow in a UK smallholding paddock. Getting the siting right before the pigs arrive saves re-doing it later: drainage, orientation, shade, and bedding volume all matter to how well the pigs settle. A guide to buying, siting, and preparing a pig arc for the first batch of weaners.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Pigs, Crown copyright. AHDB Pork, small-scale outdoor pig guides (open-access, https://ahdb.org.uk). Hugh Fearnley-Whittingstall, River Cottage Meat Book, pig-rearing chapter (reference, not copied).

glossaryTerms.definition: A piglet weaned from the sow, typically at 8 weeks old and around 15 to 20 kg liveweight. Ready to grow on to slaughter weight.
A galvanised-steel A-frame shelter, half-cylinder in cross-section, sized for 2 to 3 weaners or a single sow. Tip-resistant and hose-clean. The standard outdoor pig housing for a UK smallholding.

The natural pig behaviour of pushing the snout into soil to search for roots, insects, and tubers. Pigs will root any paddock bare within weeks; rotation and nose rings (if permitted) are the main management tools.

projectSchedule.title: Site and set up the arc and electric fencing
projectSchedule.body: Choose a well-drained spot with shade in the afternoon. Orient the arc opening away from the prevailing wind. Lay a thick bed of straw. Set the electric fence at 20 to 25 cm and 45 cm, test it reads above 3 kV, and fill the water trough.

projectSchedule.title: Check electric fence and water supply before weaners arrive
projectSchedule.body: Walk the fence line and check for vegetation touching the wire. Test the energiser output again. Confirm the water trough is full and the float valve is working. Add more straw to the arc if the bed looks thin.

projectSchedule.title: Assess ground condition and decide whether to move the arc
projectSchedule.body: After a week, check the ground around the arc entrance for poaching. If more than 15 cm of mud has built up, move the arc to a fresh spot on a grass break. Rotate the paddock before the pigs are let back on.

content.text: arrives flat-packed or assembled. Siting it takes more thought than building it. Pigs generate heat and need shade in summer; they also need a dry sleeping area and drainage away from the entrance. A poorly sited arc becomes a wet, smelly problem within two weeks of weaners arriving.

Pick a spot on level or very gently sloping ground. The entrance should face south or east, away from the prevailing wind. A north-facing entrance takes the full force of a westerly and drives rain inside. Position the arc on the higher point of the paddock if there is a slope, so drainage runs away from the sleeping area.

Shade from a tree or hedge is an advantage in summer. Pigs have no sweat glands and rely on a wallow, shade, and their sleeping area to regulate temperature. An arc in full sun in July becomes dangerously hot by midday; a shaded arc stays 10 to 15 degrees cooler inside.
Keep at least 20 metres from any watercourse or standing water. Pig slurry in a watercourse is an environmental offence. Check local land-drainage maps before siting a long-term paddock.
Use wheat straw as bedding. Barley straw is an irritant to pig skin and respiratory tracts. Put in a 20 cm deep layer before the

arrive. Pigs will move the straw to the position they prefer and sleep in a huddle. A deep bed retains heat through cold nights. Top up every three to four days; do not strip it down entirely, as the warm core of an established bed is valuable.

Pigs are contained by two strands of electric poly-wire at 20 cm and 40 cm off the ground. Pigs learn to respect an electric fence very quickly after one or two contacts; they are rarely a containment problem once trained. Training them in a small pen around the arc before giving them the full paddock makes the lesson faster.
can undermine the fence posts if pigs work close to them. Check posts weekly. A pig that has found it can root under a wire will keep doing it. Drive posts deeper in those spots or add a ground wire.

attrs.title: Training pigs to the fence
attrs.body: Set up the fence around the arc in a small area of around 4 m by 4 m before the weaners arrive. Let them into this small pen first. They will contact the fence within hours and learn to respect it. Once trained over two to three days, expand the pen to the full paddock size. An untrained pig let into a large paddock will push through the fence immediately.

content.text: Two weaners at 15 kg each drink around 5 litres of water per day, more in hot weather. A galvanised water trough filled daily or piped to a ball-float valve is the most reliable system. Position the trough outside the sleeping area; pigs drink and then wallow, making the ground wet around the trough. Site it 3 to 5 metres from the arc entrance to keep the sleeping area dry.

Check water twice daily, particularly in hot weather. Pigs that run dry in heat can deteriorate quickly. If using a ball-float valve, test it weekly and clear it of debris; pigs investigate any pipe or fitting with their snouts and block valves regularly.

items.symptom: Arc becomes waterlogged and muddy around the entrance within two weeks
items.cause: Pigs sleeping near the entrance, heavy ground, insufficient drainage away from the arc
items.fix: Scatter hardcore or wood chip around the entrance area. Move the arc onto fresh ground if the paddock allows it. Position the arc so the entrance faces downhill.

items.symptom: Pigs pushing through the electric fence repeatedly
items.cause: Fence not adequately energised, or pigs not fully trained before paddock expanded
items.fix: Check energiser output with a fence tester; a minimum of 4,000 volts is needed for reliable containment. Return to the small training pen for two more days before re-expanding.

items.symptom: Pigs refusing to use the arc in warm weather
items.cause: Arc too hot inside due to direct sun
items.fix: Create shade over the arc using a tarpaulin or shade netting suspended over the roof. Provide a wallow area (a shallow depression kept wet) outside the arc so pigs can regulate temperature outdoors.
